Senate passes infrastructure bill

By Quinn Statesman

The morning began like any other on the coast, with thin grey light over the headland and a damp breeze from the south.

By midday, however, the wind had picked up, and the lifeboat crew were already on standby for the first reports of trouble.

Witnesses described a sudden surge of seawater across the lower promenade, sweeping benches and litter bins into the harbor.

Emergency services praised the calm response of residents, who moved quickly to the higher ground above the railway line.

By evening, the coastguard had stood down most of its volunteer crews and assessment teams began their inspection of the harbor wall.

The mayor said the council would convene an emergency meeting on Monday to authorize repair funds for the damaged seafront infrastructure.

Several small businesses on the seafront reported flooding to their cellars, but most expected to reopen within a few days.

Meteorologists explained that the unusual combination of a high spring tide and an Atlantic depression had produced the worst conditions in years.
